---
title: "Template Course"
subtitle: "Week title"
format:
  live-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    brand: ../_brand.yml
    theme: ../hevs.scss
    show-slide-number: all
    preview-links: auto
    logo: https://www.hevs.ch/_nuxt/img/logo_hesso.9af1d79.svg
    footer: "{{< var title>}}"
    header-logo: "/img/exit-left.svg"
    header-logo-link: "/{{< var folder>}}/Week2/"
filters:
  - reveal-header
  - qcm
  - textanswer
  - timeline
---

## Slide 1 Title

- Point 1
- Point 2


## Images

![Example img](img/example.webp)


## Rule 2: Use Participatory Live Coding {.smaller}

> Actually type the code and narrate it as you teach. Have the participants follow along as well.

- Demonstrates your **best practices** for real workflows
- Making **mistakes** is a good thing — it shows you are human and how you **debug**
- Naturally **slows you down**, so you don't go too fast for students

Rooted in the *"I do, we do, you do"* method, pioneered for programming by [The Carpentries](https://carpentries.org/). The `{pyodide}` cells on the next slides let students follow along live, in the browser.

::: aside
Timbers, T. A., & Çetinkaya-Rundel, M. (2026). Ten simple rules for teaching data science. *PLoS Computational Biology*. [arXiv:2602.02874](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02874)
:::

## Interactive Code

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_1

# square each number
for x in range(5):
  print(______)
```

[More documentation on Live Code](https://r-wasm.github.io/quarto-live/)

## Solution Cells

Attach a hidden solution (and optional hint) to any `exercise`. A *Show solution* button appears under the editor.

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_solution
# square each number
for x in range(5):
  print(______)
```

::: { .hint exercise="ex_solution" }
Replace the blank with `x` raised to the power of two.
:::

::: { .solution exercise="ex_solution" }
```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_solution
#| solution: true
for x in range(5):
  print(x ** 2)
```
:::

::: aside
Public/student builds keep solutions; set `show-solutions: false` in the front matter to strip them.
:::

## Line Highlighting

```{.python code-line-numbers="6-8"}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(subplot_kw={'projection': 'polar'})
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()
```


## Line Higlighting with animation

```{.python code-line-numbers="|6|9"}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(subplot_kw={'projection': 'polar'})
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

## Multiple columns

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
Left column
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
Right column
:::

::::


## Aside 

- Green ^[A footnote]
- Brown
- Purple

::: aside
Some additional commentary of more peripheral interest.
:::

## Multiple Choice (QCM)

The `qcm` filter renders a self-checking, auto-shuffled question. Mark answers `\+` (correct) / `\-` (wrong).

::: {.qcm points="1" solution="true"}
### Valid Python container types?

\+ list
\+ dict
\- array (built-in)
\+ set
:::

::: aside
`solution="true"` adds a **Verify** button; `points="N"` shows in the heading.
:::

## Free-Text Answers

The `textanswer` filter renders a printable answer box for worksheets and PDF exports.

::: {.textanswer lines="3" points="3"}
### What does a list comprehension do?
:::

::: aside
`lines` sets the box height; `points` shows in the heading.
:::

## Recap Quizzes → Moodle

Each week can ship a `questions.md` file. Write questions once in plain markdown; export them to a Moodle XML question bank.

```markdown
# Week 1 — Recap

**1. What does `len(x)` return for a list?**
- *A) The number of elements
- B) The sum of the elements
- C) The largest element
```

A leading `*` marks the correct answer. Inline `code`, fenced code blocks and `![images](pic.png)` are supported (images are embedded as base64).

## Recap Quizzes → Moodle {.smaller}

Convert with the bundled script, then import the `.xml` via **Question bank → Import → Moodle XML**:

```bash
# Convert a single week
python extensions/questions_to_moodle.py Week1/questions.md

# Convert every Week*/questions.md at once
python extensions/questions_to_moodle.py --all

# Collect all XML in one folder
python extensions/questions_to_moodle.py --all --output-dir moodle_xml
```

## Jupyter Notebook Flow {.smaller}

Weekly labs are in-browser Jupyter notebooks:

1. Open the link — runs in the browser, **no install**.
2. Built-in submission form (student e-mail).
3. Work saved **locally in the browser** — reopen to resume.
4. Submit through the embedded form for grading.

::: aside
Reference solutions → PDF via `extensions/convert_solutions.py`.
:::

## Incremental Lists

Add `.incremental` to reveal list items one click at a time.

::: {.incremental}
- First point appears
- Then the second
- Finally the third
:::

::: aside
Set `incremental: true` in the front matter to make it the default for every list.
:::

## Highlight the Current Item {.smaller}

Combine `.incremental` with `.highlight-last` to dim earlier points and keep focus on the one just revealed.

::: {.incremental .highlight-last}
- Collect the data
- Clean and explore it
- Model and evaluate
- Communicate the result
:::

## Timeline

The [`timeline`](https://github.com/EmilHvitfeldt/quarto-timeline) filter renders events along a line. Each `.event` has a `data-label`.

:::: {.timeline .tl-compact}
::: {.event data-label="Week 1" .fragment}
Python & tooling
:::
::: {.event data-label="Week 2" .fragment}
Collecting data
:::
::: {.event data-label="Week 3" .fragment}
Visualisation
:::
::: {.event data-label="…" .fragment}
Group project
:::
::::

## Arrows

The `arrow` shortcode draws curved SVG arrows to annotate slides.

Cause {{< arrow from="0,12" to="70,12" color="#009efa" >}} Effect

Use `position="absolute"` with pixel coordinates to point anywhere on the slide:

{{< arrow from="250,250" to="650,180" color="#6cc757" curve="0.4" width="3" label="look here!" position="absolute" >}}

::: aside
Install with `quarto add EmilHvitfeldt/quarto-arrows`. Options include `curve`, `bend`, `dash`, `label`, and `fragment` for draw-on-click.
:::

## Iframe

Embed live web content (polls, dashboards, demos):

<iframe allowfullscreen frameborder="0" height="500" mozallowfullscreen style="min-width: 500px;" src="https://app.wooclap.com/events/TDLGXL/questions/690b2ee5f285fa1af30e2001" width="100%"></iframe>

::: aside
Interactive iframes grab keyboard focus, so keep them on the **last** slide — otherwise arrow-key navigation can get trapped here.
:::
